response.description_long:
Rowfern applies pull-request discipline to data pipelines. The failures it targets are the quiet ones — broken schemas, silent nulls, volume anomalies — that surface in dashboards instead of code review, and its stated ambition is to move the catch from the postmortem to the pull request.

The core object is the contract, not the check. Rowfern parses a dbt project's lineage and generates schema contracts at the boundaries between producing and consuming models: column names, types, nullability, accepted values. A pull request that changes a producer runs the contract check for every downstream consumer, listing the blast radius on the PR — the column rename that would have silently nulled a dashboard becomes a red check before merge. Freshness and volume expectations calibrate from history and recalibrate with seasonality, and hand-written YAML assertions cover business logic, with severity levels deciding whether a failure annotates, blocks merge, or pages.

Checks run in CI against a sampled snapshot of production data, typically finishing in under five minutes, then run again on schedule in production — one definition of correctness in both places — paging the owning team through PagerDuty or Slack with the diff and downstream consumers attached. Warehouse coverage spans Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, and Databricks, and checks execute inside the warehouse or over a read-only connection; sampled data never leaves the environment unless cloud snapshots are opted into. The platform is SOC 2 Type II certified.

The business is open core: the check engine is Apache 2.0 — contract generation, the check library, CI integration — while the commercial platform adds the browsable contract catalog, ownership routing, historical baselines, SSO, and audit logs. Cloud plans start at $500 per month per workspace and scale with models under contract, with unlimited users and runs.

Traction is unusually specific for a 19-person company: data platform teams at Chartbeam, Densify Analytics, and Copperline Health run Rowfern on more than 4,000 models. Chartbeam gates every dbt PR on contracts after a metrics regression reached a customer-facing dashboard, and the site is candid that teams adopt after a visible outage — a broken dashboard in a board meeting being the common origin story.

Rowfern was founded in Denver in 2022 by former data engineers Owen Park and Talia Brooks; the team is 19 people, remote across US time zones, including the open-source engine's maintainers. The wedge is a workflow claim: that data teams will review pipeline changes the way software teams review code, with the contract as the enforcement point.
